messages.content: You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I’m so tired of this daily chore. Every time a new client inquiry lands in my inbox, I have to open the email, find the sender’s name, the subject line, and that little amount they mention, then copy it all into my tracking spreadsheet. It’s taking up way too much time.
Aria (AI): That sounds repetitive—definitely something we can automate. Just to make sure I understand, is it *every* email in your inbox, or only ones from specific clients or with certain keywords in the subject?
Natalie: Oh, good question! Only the ones with ‘New Client Inquiry’ in the subject. And they’re always from the same domain—@prospectpartners.com. The amount is usually in the first sentence, like ‘We’d like to discuss a project budget of $5,000.’
Aria (AI): Got it. So we’ll watch for emails from @prospectpartners.com with ‘New Client Inquiry’ in the subject. Now, where do you want this data to go? Is it an existing spreadsheet, or should we create a new one?
Natalie: It’s my existing one—‘Client Tracker 2024’. The sheet is called ‘Inquiries’. I just need the sender’s email, the subject line, and that amount pulled into the next empty row.
Aria (AI): Perfect. One last thing—do you want me to include the date and time the email arrived, or is that not necessary?
Natalie: Oh, yes! That’d be super helpful. I always forget to add that manually.
Aria (AI): Let me make sure I’ve got this right: when an email arrives from @prospectpartners.com with ‘New Client Inquiry’ in the subject, we’ll pull the sender’s email, subject, amount mentioned, and the timestamp, then add it all to the next empty row in your ‘Inquiries’ sheet. Does that cover everything?
Natalie: Yes! That’s exactly what I need. Thank you!

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, Fridays are the worst because of this stupid invoice report I have to send. Every single week, I spend like an hour pulling together all the overdue invoices from that big spreadsheet and then formatting them into an email for the finance team. There’s got to be a better way, right?
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds like such a drag! I can definitely help you automate this. Let’s start with the basics—where are you pulling the overdue invoices from? Is it a Google Sheet?
Natalie: Yeah, it’s a Google Sheet. It’s called ‘Client Invoices 2024,’ and the tab I use is named ‘Overdue.’ I just filter for anything that’s past due and copy the rows into an email.
Aria (AI): Got it. And what columns do you usually include in the email? Like, do you just grab the client name, invoice number, amount, and due date?
Natalie: Yeah, those are the main ones. But I also add a little note at the top saying something like, ‘Here are the overdue invoices as of [today’s date].’ Oh, and I always bold the amount column in the email so it stands out.
Aria (AI): That makes sense. And who do you send this email to? Is it always the same person or a group, like ‘finance-team@company.com’?
Natalie: It’s always the finance team email, and I CC our manager, Sarah. The subject line is usually something like ‘Weekly Overdue Invoices – [Date].’
Aria (AI): Perfect. Let me make sure I’ve got this right: Every Friday, you want to automatically pull the overdue invoices from the ‘Overdue’ tab in the ‘Client Invoices 2024’ sheet, format them into an email with the client name, invoice number, amount (bolded), and due date, add a note at the top with today’s date, and send it to the finance team with Sarah CC’d. Does that cover everything?
Natalie: Yes! That’s exactly it. And if it could just run on its own every Friday morning, that would save me so much time.
Aria (AI): Great! I’ll set this up for you. You’ll have a workflow ready to go—no more manual copying and pasting. I’ll send you the details in a sec.

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I hate this part of my morning. Every single day, I have to go through all these client enquiry emails and manually type them into our tracking sheet. It’s such a waste of time, and I always worry I’ll mess up a number or miss something important.
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds tedious—and stressful! How many emails are we talking about on an average morning?
Natalie: Usually around 10 to 15. Sometimes more if it’s a busy week. And each one has like five or six details I need to pull out: name, company, phone number, what they’re asking about, and the date they sent it.
Aria (AI): Got it. And do all these enquiry emails come from the same place, or do you have to check multiple inboxes?
Natalie: No, they all come to our main info@ email address. That’s the one I check every morning.
Aria (AI): Perfect. And just to confirm, the Google Sheet you’re adding them to—is that the same one every time, or does it change?
Natalie: Same one. It’s called ‘Client Enquiries 2024’ and the tab is just ‘Log’. The columns are already set up: Date, Client Name, Company, Phone, Enquiry Type, and Notes.
Aria (AI): Okay, let me make sure I’ve got this right. Every morning, you want to automatically pull all new emails from info@yourcompany.com that are client enquiries, extract the key details like name, company, and phone number, and add them as a new row in your ‘Client Enquiries 2024’ sheet under the ‘Log’ tab. Does that cover it?
Natalie: Yes! Exactly. And if it could just skip any emails that aren’t actually enquiries—like newsletters or spam—that would be amazing.
Aria (AI): No problem, I can set it up to only look for emails with ‘enquiry’ or ‘question’ in the subject line. I’ll get this workflow ready for you—it should save you at least 30 minutes every morning!

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, Aria, I'm drowning in job applications! Every time I get an email from a candidate, I have to open it, copy all their details, and paste them into our tracking spreadsheet. It's taking forever, and I keep worrying I'll miss something important.
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds exhausting! I can definitely help with that. First, tell me—do all the application emails come from the same place, or is it a mix of different senders?
Natalie: It's mostly from our careers inbox, careers@ourcompany.com, but sometimes candidates email me directly too. The subject line usually says 'Application for [Job Title]' though.
Aria (AI): Got it. And what kind of details do you need to pull from each email? Like their name, email, the job they're applying for, and...?
Natalie: Yeah, exactly! Their name, email, phone number, the job title they're applying for, and their resume attachment. Oh, and the date they applied—sometimes that's in the email, but if not, just today's date is fine.
Aria (AI): Perfect. And where do you want all this info to go? Is it that 'Job Applications Tracker' spreadsheet you shared with me last week?
Natalie: Yes! That's the one. It has columns for all the stuff I mentioned—Name, Email, Phone, Job Title, Resume Link, and Application Date.
Aria (AI): Great. Just to make sure I’ve got this right: whenever an email comes in from careers@ourcompany.com *or* has 'Application for' in the subject, we’ll grab the candidate’s name, email, phone, job title, and resume, then add a new row to the tracker with all that info. Does that sound right?
Natalie: Yes! That’s exactly what I need. Oh, and if the email doesn’t have a phone number, just leave that cell blank. Same with the resume—if there’s no attachment, just skip it.
Aria (AI): Got it—I’ll make sure it handles missing info gracefully. This should save you at least 15 minutes per application. Let me set that up for you now.

Generate the workflow JSON.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I am so tired of this. Every time I get a new job application email, I have to open my spreadsheet and type in the sender’s name, the subject line, and the salary they’re asking for. It’s taking up so much of my time, and I keep making little mistakes.
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds really tedious! How many of these emails do you get in a typical week?
Natalie: Probably around 20 or so. It’s not a ton, but it adds up, especially when I’m trying to focus on other stuff.
Aria (AI): Got it. And is there a specific subject line or sender these emails usually have? Like, do they all come from the same job board or something?
Natalie: Yeah, actually! They all have ‘Job Application’ in the subject line, and most of them come from this one domain—applicants@hireme.com. A few might come from other places, but those are rare.
Aria (AI): Okay, that’s helpful. And just to confirm, you’re only pulling out the sender’s name, the subject line, and the salary they mention, right? Is there anything else you’d want to grab from the email?
Natalie: Hmm, maybe the date they sent it? Sometimes I need to sort by that. But the other three are the big ones.
Aria (AI): Perfect. And what’s the name of the spreadsheet where you’re adding this info? Is it the same one every time?
Natalie: Yeah, it’s called ‘Job Applications Tracker.’ I’ve got a tab called ‘New Submissions’ where I dump all the new ones.
Aria (AI): Let me make sure I’ve got this right: whenever an email comes in with ‘Job Application’ in the subject, from applicants@hireme.com or anyone else, you want to pull out the sender’s name, subject line, salary, and the date it was sent. Then add all that to the ‘New Submissions’ tab in your ‘Job Applications Tracker’ spreadsheet. Does that sound right?
Natalie: Yes! Exactly. That would save me so much time.
Aria (AI): Great! I’ll set that up for you. It should take just a second to get this workflow ready.

Generate the workflow JSON.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, Fridays are the worst for me. I have to pull together this sales summary from our team spreadsheet and email it to the managers. It takes forever to format it nicely, and I always feel like I'm forgetting something.
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds tedious! I can definitely help automate that for you. First, could you tell me which spreadsheet you pull the data from? Is it the same one every week?
Natalie: Yeah, it's the 'Weekly Sales Tracker' sheet in our shared team folder. I usually just copy the numbers from the 'Summary' tab.
Aria (AI): Got it. And what exactly do you include in the email? Just the raw numbers, or do you add any commentary or formatting?
Natalie: Oh, I always add a little note at the top like 'Here's this week's sales update!' and then I bold the total sales number so it stands out. The managers like that.
Aria (AI): That makes sense. Last question—who do you send this email to every week? Is it always the same group of people?
Natalie: Yep, it's always the same three managers: Sarah, Mark, and Lisa. I just cc our team lead, Jamie, too.
Aria (AI): Perfect. Let me make sure I've got this right: every Friday, you want to pull the latest sales data from the 'Summary' tab of the 'Weekly Sales Tracker' spreadsheet, format it with a little note and bold the total, then email it to Sarah, Mark, Lisa, and cc Jamie. Does that sound right?
Natalie: Yes! That's exactly it. And if it could just send automatically at like 4 PM on Fridays, that would be amazing.
Aria (AI): Great! I'll set that up for you. This should save you about an hour every week. I'll generate the workflow now.

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, Aria, I need your help with something. Every time I'm stuck in back-to-back meetings, I come back to a mountain of customer emails, and I never know which ones are actually urgent. I feel like I'm always playing catch-up.
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds stressful! I totally get why you'd want to fix that. Let me ask—what kinds of emails usually need your immediate attention when you do get to them?
Natalie: Well, if the subject line says 'URGENT' or 'Payment Failed,' those are the big ones. Also, if it's from our VIP clients—like anyone from Acme Corp or Globex Industries. Those can't wait.
Aria (AI): Got it. So priority is based on the subject line *and* who it's from. How do you usually handle these urgent emails when you spot them?
Natalie: I forward them to my team lead, Mark, and cc our support Slack channel so everyone’s in the loop. But I also keep a log of all urgent requests in a Google Sheet—just the date, customer name, and a quick note about the issue.
Aria (AI): That makes sense. Just to confirm—do you want this workflow to *only* handle the urgent emails, or should it also log the non-urgent ones somewhere for later?
Natalie: Oh, good question! Let’s just focus on the urgent ones for now. I can handle the rest when I’m not swamped.
Aria (AI): Perfect. And one last thing—how often do you check that Google Sheet where you log the urgent requests? Is it something you update daily, or more like a real-time thing?
Natalie: It’s more real-time. I add to it as soon as I see the email, so it’s always up to date.
Aria (AI): Let me make sure I’ve got this right: When an email comes in with 'URGENT' or 'Payment Failed' in the subject *or* it’s from Acme Corp or Globex Industries, you want Aria to automatically forward it to Mark and your Slack channel, and also add a row to your Google Sheet with the date, customer name, and a note about the issue. Does that cover everything?
Natalie: Yes! That’s exactly what I need. Thank you so much, Aria!
Aria (AI): You’re welcome, Natalie! I’ll set that up for you right away. Give me just a second to generate the workflow.

Generate the workflow JSON.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I’m so tired of sending the same email over and over. Every time a supplier sends us an invoice or a quote, I have to reply with that same boring ‘Got it, thanks!’ message. It’s such a waste of time.
Aria (AI): I totally get it, Natalie. That sounds like a repetitive task that could use a little automation magic. Just to make sure I understand—do these emails always come from the same suppliers, or is it anyone who sends you an invoice or quote?
Natalie: Oh, it’s mostly the same suppliers. Like, 90% of the time it’s from Acme Corp, Global Supplies, or TechParts. But sometimes we get one-off quotes from new vendors, too.
Aria (AI): Got it. And do you want to auto-reply to *all* of them, or just the ones from those three main suppliers? Also, is there a specific subject line these emails usually have, like ‘Invoice’ or ‘Quote’?
Natalie: Hmm, good question. I think I’d want to auto-reply to the main suppliers—Acme, Global Supplies, and TechParts. The others I can handle manually. And yeah, the subject usually has ‘Invoice’ or ‘Quote’ in it, like ‘Invoice #12345’ or ‘Quote for Project X.’
Aria (AI): Perfect. Last thing—what should the auto-reply say? Do you want to keep it simple like ‘Got it, thanks!’ or add anything else, like a reference to the invoice number or a note about next steps?
Natalie: Oh, just keep it simple. Something like, ‘Hi there, thanks for sending this over! We’ve received your invoice/quote and will review it shortly. Let us know if you need anything else.’ But maybe pull in the invoice or quote number from the subject line so it feels a little more personal?
Aria (AI): That makes sense. Let me make sure I’ve got this right: You want an auto-reply sent to emails from Acme Corp, Global Supplies, or TechParts, where the subject contains ‘Invoice’ or ‘Quote.’ The reply will say, ‘Hi there, thanks for sending this over! We’ve received your [invoice/quote] and will review it shortly. Let us know if you need anything else,’ with the invoice or quote number pulled from the subject. Does that sound right?
Natalie: Yes, exactly! That’s perfect.
Aria (AI): Great! I’ll set that up for you right now.

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I’m so tired of dealing with these contractor invoices every week. It’s the same thing over and over—read the email, figure out who it’s for, then either reply or forward it. There’s got to be a better way, right?
Aria (AI): Oh, absolutely! I can set up something to handle this for you. First, do these invoices always come from the same email addresses, or is it different contractors each time?
Natalie: Mostly the same ones, yeah. Like, we have three main contractors—Acme Design, Bright Solutions, and QuickBuild. But sometimes a new one pops up, and I have to forward those to accounting to get them set up in the system.
Aria (AI): Got it. And when you say you ‘figure out who it’s for,’ do you mean you’re checking the subject line or the body of the email to decide where it goes?
Natalie: Yeah, usually the subject line. Like, if it’s from Acme Design, it’s always for Sarah in marketing. Bright Solutions is for Tom in operations, and QuickBuild is for me—I handle those myself. But if it’s a new contractor, I just forward it to accounting@ourcompany.com.
Aria (AI): That makes sense. And when you handle QuickBuild invoices yourself, what do you do with them? Do you just reply to acknowledge receipt, or do you log them somewhere?
Natalie: Oh, I log them in this Google Sheet we have—it’s called ‘Contractor Invoices’ and there’s a tab for ‘QuickBuild.’ I just add the date, invoice number, and amount. It’s super simple, but it helps me keep track.
Aria (AI): Perfect. And for the others—like Sarah and Tom—do you just forward the email as-is, or do you add any notes?
Natalie: I usually just forward it, but sometimes I add a quick note like ‘FYI, this is for the Q3 campaign’ or whatever. But honestly, 90% of the time, it’s just a straight forward.
Aria (AI): Okay, let me make sure I’ve got this right. When an invoice comes in, you want me to check if it’s from one of the three main contractors. If it’s Acme Design, forward to Sarah; Bright Solutions, forward to Tom; QuickBuild, log the details in your sheet and reply to acknowledge. If it’s from anyone else, forward to accounting. Does that cover everything?
Natalie: Yes! That’s exactly it. And if I could add a little note when forwarding to Sarah or Tom, that’d be great—like ‘Automated forward: invoice for your review.’ Just so they know it’s not me doing it manually.
Aria (AI): Got it! I’ll add that note. Give me just a second to set this up for you...

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I keep missing supplier emails when I'm stuck in back-to-back meetings. By the time I see them, it's like hours later and I feel so rude not replying right away.
Aria (AI): That sounds stressful! Would it help if we set up something to automatically acknowledge those emails for you when they come in?
Natalie: Oh my gosh, yes! But only for the suppliers though - I don't want to auto-reply to everything. How would that work?
Aria (AI): Great point! We'll make sure it only responds to supplier emails. Do you get emails from specific supplier domains, or do they usually have something in the subject line?
Natalie: Most of them come from @suppliercorp.com or @partsunlimited.net. And they usually have 'PO' or 'Purchase Order' in the subject.
Aria (AI): Got it. And what would you like the automatic reply to say? Should it be the same for all suppliers, or different based on the supplier?
Natalie: Just a standard message is fine. Something like 'Thanks for your email! We've received your purchase order and will process it within 24 hours.'
Aria (AI): Perfect. One last thing - should we also log these emails somewhere, like in a spreadsheet, so you can keep track of them?
Natalie: Oh, that would be amazing! I have a 'Supplier Communications' spreadsheet where I track all these. The sheet is called 'Incoming POs'.
Aria (AI): Let me make sure I've got this right: We'll set up an automatic reply to emails from @suppliercorp.com or @partsunlimited.net with 'PO' in the subject, send your standard acknowledgment, and log the email details in your 'Incoming POs' sheet. Does that cover everything?
Natalie: Yes! That sounds perfect. Thank you so much - this is going to save me so much time and stress.

Generate the workflow JSON.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, I’m so tired of doing this every Friday. I have to go into the weekly sales tracker, copy all the new entries from this week, and then email the team with a recap. It’s such a waste of time!
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds tedious! How often do you do this—just once a week?
Natalie: Yeah, every Friday afternoon. The team expects the update by 4 PM so they can review it over the weekend if needed.
Aria (AI): Got it. And where are the new entries in the spreadsheet? Is there a specific column that marks them as ‘new’ or do you just look at the dates?
Natalie: Oh, good question! There’s a ‘Date Added’ column, and I usually filter for anything from Monday to Friday of the current week. The entries are in columns A through E—customer name, product, quantity, sale amount, and notes.
Aria (AI): Perfect. And what about the email—do you always send it to the same group? Is there a specific subject line or format you use?
Natalie: Yeah, it’s always the same group: the sales team and our manager. The subject line is usually something like ‘Weekly Sales Recap – [Date Range]’. The body just lists the new entries in a table and includes a quick summary at the top, like ‘Here’s what came in this week!’
Aria (AI): Got it. One last thing—do you ever need to double-check anything before sending, or is it pretty straightforward once the data is pulled?
Natalie: Nope, it’s straightforward. I just make sure the numbers look right, but I don’t need to add anything extra.
Aria (AI): Let me make sure I’ve got this right: Every Friday, you want to automatically pull all new entries from the ‘Weekly Sales Tracker’ spreadsheet (filtering by this week’s dates in the ‘Date Added’ column), format them into a table, and email the sales team and your manager with a subject like ‘Weekly Sales Recap – [Date Range]’. Does that sound right?
Natalie: Yes! Exactly. That would save me so much time.
Aria (AI): Great! I’ll set that up for you. You’ll never have to manually copy and paste again.

Generate the workflow JSON.

You are an expert workflow automation builder. Given a conversation transcript between a user and an AI assistant, output a valid workflow JSON that automates the task described. Output only valid JSON, nothing else.

Here is the conversation transcript:

Natalie: Ugh, Aria, I’m so tired of this daily chore. Every time a new job application lands in my inbox, I have to open it, copy the candidate’s name, email, the position they applied for, and their resume link, then paste it all into our tracking spreadsheet. It’s mind-numbing and takes forever—especially when we get a bunch at once.
Aria (AI): Oh no, that sounds like a total time-suck! How many applications do you usually get in a day? Like, is this something that happens a few times a week or more like a dozen times a day?
Natalie: Oh, it varies. On a slow day, maybe 5 or 6. But when we post a new role? Easily 20 or 30. It’s not just the time—it’s the risk of typos or missing something important because I’m rushing.
Aria (AI): Got it. So, just to make sure I’m on the right track—you want to automatically pull out the candidate’s name, email, the job title they applied for, and the link to their resume from each email, right? Is there anything else you’d want to grab, like a cover letter or phone number?
Natalie: Oh, phone number would be great! And if it’s not too much trouble, sometimes the email includes a note like ‘referred by so-and-so’—could we grab that too? But honestly, the name, email, job title, and resume link are the big ones.
Aria (AI): Perfect, that’s super helpful. Now, do all these applications come from the same email address, like applications@yourcompany.com, or do they come from different senders?
Natalie: They all come from our careers portal, so it’s always from careers@ourcompany.com. The subject line always starts with ‘New Application:’ followed by the job title, like ‘New Application: Marketing Manager.’
Aria (AI): That’s great—it’ll make it easier to spot the right emails. One last thing: what’s the name of the spreadsheet where you track all this? And is there a specific tab or range where you want the data added?
Natalie: It’s called ‘2024 Applicant Tracker,’ and I want the data to go into the ‘New Applications’ tab. The columns are: Name, Email, Phone, Position, Resume Link, and Notes. Oh, and if the ‘Notes’ field could include that referral info, that’d be amazing.
Aria (AI): Let me make sure I’ve got this right: You want me to watch your inbox for emails from careers@ourcompany.com with a subject line starting with ‘New Application:’. When one arrives, I’ll pull out the candidate’s name, email, phone number, the job title, the resume link, and any referral info. Then I’ll add all that to the ‘New Applications’ tab in your ‘2024 Applicant Tracker’ spreadsheet. Does that sound right?
Natalie: Yes! That’s exactly it. How long do you think this will save me every week?
Aria (AI): Based on what you’ve told me, I’d estimate this could save you around 2 to 3 hours every week—maybe even more when you’re swamped with applications. I’ll go ahead and set that up for you now.

Generate the workflow JSON.
